description: Figure 2 presents the temperature-dependent tensile deformation behavior of as-forged Fe-20Mn-6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel tested at 25°C, -77°C, and -196°C. Subfigure (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves showing simultaneous increases in strength and ductility as temperature decreases, with ultimate tensile strength rising from approximately 800 MPa at 25°C to 1300 MPa at -196°C and elongation increasing from 32% to 68%. Subfigure (b) illustrates true stress-strain curves (solid lines) and work hardening rate curves (dashed lines), revealing that cryogenic temperatures exhibit near-linear work hardening behavior compared to continuous decrease at room temperature, with the intersection indicating necking points. Subfigure (c) quantifies linear temperature dependence: yield strength and ultimate tensile strength follow YS=474.6-2.05×T and UTS=813.0-2.36×T (T in °C), while total elongation and uniform elongation increase with decreasing temperature. These results demonstrate anomalous cryogenic strengthening and toughening attributed to decreasing stacking fault energy promoting planar glide and mechanical twinning.
